enum.color: The 16 fixed C64 colours. Usable anywhere a colour is asked for — you write the name, not the number.
gfx.text: Text mode — also the tilemap engine (font = tileset).
gfx.bitmap: Bitmap mode — freely painted pixels instead of tiles.
gfx.hires: High resolution: fine pixels, sparing with colour.
gfx.multicolor: Multicolour: coarser pixels, but more colourful.
cmd.graphics: Sets the stage: picks which mode the screen runs in. Belongs at the very start, before anything visible happens.
param.graphics.area: The screen's mode — TEXT (the tilemap engine: letters & tiles) or BITMAP (freely painted pixels).
param.graphics.colormode: How many colours per dot: HIRES (fine, fewer colours) or MULTICOLOR (coarser, but more colourful).
cmd.cls: Wipes the inner play area and floods it with one colour in a single stroke. It leaves the border alone — and sweeps away whatever is already on stage, so call it before the show begins.
param.cls.color: The colour the cleared area is filled with.
cmd.bordercolor: Colours the thin frame around the play area. It's a loner with its own colour — Cls can't reach it.
cmd.color: Sets the pen colour for following text (later lines & rectangles too). Stays until you change it.
param.color.color: The colour written with from now on.
cmd.usesprite: Places a painted sprite into one of the 8 slots (0–7). Baked in at build time, not loaded from disk.
param.usesprite.slot: Which of the 8 sprite slots (0–7) to fill.
param.usesprite.id: Name of the sprite graphic from your project.
cmd.usetileset: Loads a character set as your tile palette — 256 tiles of 8×8 pixels to build your world from.
param.usetileset.id: Name of the tileset from your project.
cmd.useimage: Makes a full-screen image ready (bitmap mode).
param.useimage.id: Name of the image from your project.
cmd.drawimage: Paints a whole image to the screen — expensive, so for setup, not every frame.
param.drawimage.id: Name of the image from your project.
cmd.text: Writes text at a spot: column first, then row, then the text. Cheap in text mode, expensive in bitmap mode.
cmd.drawmap: Builds a whole painted map onto the screen — all 1000 cells at once. Expensive: build the static world once, not every frame.
param.drawmap.id: Name of the map from your project.
kw.if: Lets something happen only when a condition holds. Multi-line with EndIf, single-line with Then.
kw.then: The quick one-liner form: If <condition> Then <one statement> — no EndIf needed.
kw.elseif: Tests another condition when the previous one didn't hold.
kw.else: The otherwise case: runs when none of the conditions held.
kw.select: Branches on the value of an expression into several cases — tidier than many ElseIf.
kw.case: A case inside Select: matches when the value fits.
kw.default: The catch-all case in Select when no Case matches.
kw.while: Repeats the block while the condition holds. Closed with Wend.
kw.repeat: Repeats the block until the condition at the end (Until) holds — runs at least once.
kw.until: The stop condition at the end of a Repeat loop.
kw.for: Counts a variable from one value to another, repeating the block. Closed with Next.
kw.next: Closes a For loop and advances the counter.
kw.to: Names the upper bound of the counting loop: For i = 1 To 10.
kw.step: Optional step size of the counting loop; without Step the step is 1.
kw.exit: Breaks out of the running loop at once — the emergency exit for all three loop kinds.
kw.function: Begins your own function. If the name carries a suffix (.b/.w/$) it returns a value; without one it's a statement. Closed with EndFunction.
kw.return: Returns a value from a function and ends it.
kw.global: Declares a variable visible everywhere — with a mandatory initial value: Global score.w = 0.
kw.const: Gives a fixed number a name. Costs nothing at runtime — the value is inlined.
kw.dim: Declares a fixed-length list, 1- or 2-dimensional: Dim field.b[40,25]. Counting starts at 0.
kw.data: Stores fixed rows of values in the program — costs nothing at runtime.
kw.read: Reads the next value from the Data rows.
kw.type: Builds your own blueprint from several fields that belong together. Closed with EndType.
kw.field: A single field inside a Type blueprint.
kw.include: Pulls more CRUMB source into your program. Plain text inclusion, costs nothing at runtime.
cmd.restore: Resets the Data read pointer back to the start (or to a label).
param.restore.label: Optional label to resume reading from; without it, from the start.
cmd.end: Ends the game cleanly and hands the C64 back to BASIC.
cmd.settile: Sets a single 8×8 cell by tile number and colour — the counterpart to Sprite for the tile world. For moving enemies or growing plants.
cmd.setmetatile: Sets a MetaTile composition defined in the editor (several chars, e.g. 2×2 = 16×16) as one unit. Column/row is the top-left corner; the colours come from the MetaTile.
param.setmetatile.id: Name of the MetaTile from your tileset.
fn.gettile: Reads the tile number of a cell. Layer 0 is what's visible, layer 1 an invisible data layer beneath — the basis of the "latent object".
fn.getmetatile: Returns the identity (name) of the MetaTile at a cell — even when you query an inner cell. This is how you ask "what's there?".
fn.tilesolid: Tells whether the tile at a pixel position is marked solid — for bouncing off and standing on. Pixel-based, matches sprite coordinates.
fn.tileat: Returns the raw tile number at a pixel position.
cmd.sprite: Positions a sprite (0–7) — the VIC chip draws it in hardware, you just say where. The form Sprite n, OFF switches it off.
cmd.hidesprite: Hides a sprite without forgetting its position.
fn.spritehit: Tells whether two sprites touch — coarsely: only THAT, not which side (a C64 hardware bit).
fn.joystick: Reads a joystick direction or the fire button — true while it's held. No axis values, that's C64-honest.
param.joystick.dir: Direction or button: LEFT, RIGHT, UP, DOWN or FIRE.
fn.keydown: True while a key is held down — for continuous actions.
fn.keyhit: True exactly once per key press — for one-shot triggers.
cmd.vwait: Waits for the next frame, keeping a calm, even pace. Mandatory in the main loop — leave it out and BreadCraft nudges you.
fn.abs: The absolute value of a number — the minus sign drops away.
fn.random: A random number from 0 to n-1.
fn.len: The length of a string (number of characters).
fn.left: The first n characters of a string.
param.left.n: How many characters from the left.
fn.right: The last n characters of a string.
param.right.n: How many characters from the right.
fn.mid: A slice from the middle: n characters starting at position p.
fn.find: The position where a substring occurs in a string. Cost grows with length (a scan).
fn.int: Converts a string into a number. Nonsense text becomes 0.
fn.str: Converts a number into a string. Size the target buffer generously.
fn.chr: A one-character string from a PETSCII code: Chr$(193) = "A". Pairs with Asc.
fn.asc: The PETSCII code of the first character — C64 reality, not ASCII: Asc("A") = 193. Pairs with Chr$.
kw.asm: The hardware valve: raw 6502 assembler for what the language deliberately leaves out (such as more than 8 sprites). BreadCraft's protection ends here — cost and safety are entirely up to you. Closed with EndAsm.
op.mul: Multiplies. Free with a power of two (a shift), otherwise a bit pricier.
op.div: Divides. Expensive on the 6502 — in a frame BreadCraft warns and suggests a table.
op.mod: The remainder of a division. Expensive like the division itself.
op.and: Logical and: both conditions must hold.
op.or: Logical or: at least one condition holds.
op.xor: Exclusive or: exactly one side holds.
op.not: Flips a condition to its opposite.
op.shl: Shifts the bits left — a fast times-power-of-two.
op.shr: Shifts the bits right — a fast divide-by-power-of-two.
type.byte: A small whole number from 0 to 255. Needs the least space.
type.word: A whole number from 0 to 65535 — for larger values like a high score.
type.sint: A signed number from −32768 to 32767 — the only type allowed to go negative (e.g. velocities).
type.strbuf: A fixed-size string buffer (N characters) — mutable, so for text that changes.
type.strconst: A string assigned directly from a literal and not changed afterwards (a constant).
enum.joydir: Joystick directions and fire button for the joystick query.
enum.key: Keyboard constants for KeyDown/KeyHit (with the KEY_ prefix).
enum.spritestate: State for the form Sprite n, OFF.
